text: The surgeon begins the procedure by carefully dissecting and removing omental adhesions from the deep pelvis area.
The surgeon carefully retracts the omentum from the pelvis, ensuring minimal distortion of surrounding tissue planes.
The surgeon points to the portion of the omentum connected to the fistula near the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon visually demonstrates the colovaginal fistula, highlighting its anatomical location and appearance.
The surgeon inspects the deeper pelvis, which appears completely clean and free of any visible adhesions or obstructions.
The surgeon proceeds to take down the fistula, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues in the pelvic region.
The surgeon points to the fistula, highlighting its location in relation to the surrounding tissues.
A small abscess in the cervix is visible and drains spontaneously.
The surgeon points out the defect in the colon, highlighting the area of concern within the surgical field.
The surgeon marks the distal aspect of the proximal bowel, indicating the safe location for anastomosis.
The surgeon carefully separates the remaining colon from the left pelvic side wall, initiating a medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon enters the retroperitoneal plane, revealing the anatomical space behind the peritoneum during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery, carefully dissecting it from surrounding tissues in preparation for further surgical steps.
The surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the colon, which appears non-redundant, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon takes down the splenic flexure, detaching it from surrounding tissues as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the colon, following a standard approach.
The surgeon visually isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery, highlighting its position within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the isolated superior hemorrhoidal vessel.
The surgeon completes additional medial dissection before initiating lateral dissection of the colon, progressing with the mobilization process.
The surgeon demonstrates the limited proximal mobilization of a non-redundant colon, highlighting the anatomical configuration and tissue manipulation.
The surgeon follows the white line along the colon, indicating the anatomical boundary for dissection during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon aims to connect the previously medially dissected plane, visible beneath the current dissection area.
The ureter is visible in the surgical field, identified as a distinct tubular structure running along the dissection plane.
The surgeon joins the previously medially dissected plane, visually connecting the tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the mid-sigmoid colon, which is adhered to the left side wall, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, including the mobilization of the splenic flexure, ensuring the colon is freed from its lateral attachments.
The surgeon divides the distal mesentery, separating it from the surrounding tissues.
The surgical procedure is performed using four 8mm ports, which are visibly positioned for access to the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon marks the margins for transection using cautery after assessing perfusion with indocyanine green (ICG).
The surgeon proceeds to divide the proximal section of the colon using surgical instruments, following the marked margins for transection.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the distal section of the colon using surgical instruments, following the marking of margins for transection.
The surgeon inserts an EEA sizer into the vagina, preparing for the next step in the procedure.
The surgeon performs a posterior vaginotomy, making an incision at the posterior aspect of the vaginal wall.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through the previously created opening, completing the removal process.
The surgeon visually inspects the posterior vaginotomy site, observing the incision and surrounding tissue.
The cervix is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a sewn bowel anastomosis, meticulously stitching the bowel ends together to create a continuous passage.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-Loc suture to create the inner layer of the anastomosis, starting posteriorly and running around the sides of the bowel.
The surgeon begins to canal the anterior lip of the bowel after rounding the corners.
The surgeon completes the anterior inner row of sutures, finalizing the closure of the inner layer.
The surgeon uses a tua PDS suture to create an outer anterior row, running it down to the loop of the posterior outer row, preparing to tie the two rows together.
The surgeon prepares to perform a leak test on the newly constructed anastomosis, checking for any signs of leakage at the surgical site.
The proximal bowel is visibly distended, indicating adequate inflation during the procedure.
The surgeon fills the pelvis with a substantial amount of omentum and sutures it in place, ensuring secure positioning within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon places a residual suture through the omentum, directing it towards the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon visually inspects the anastomosis, ensuring it remains separate from the vaginal closure.
The surgeon places omentum directly into the defect in the cervix and sutures it in place, filling the area with the omental tissue.
